content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 3, 1991


L. Michael Bogert
Office of Assemblymember Ross Johnson
424 W. Commonwealth
Fullerton, California 92632

Re: Your Request For Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-90-731

Dear Mr. Bogert:

This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice I gave you on November 29, 1990 concerning
Assemblymember Ross Johnson's duties under the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1

Specifically, you asked:

(1) May a legislator establish a ballot measure committee to
defend Proposition 73 in litigation?

(2) May Assemblymember Johnson transfer campaign funds to
the ballot measure committee for the purpose of reimbursing
Senator Quentin Kopp for Proposition 73 litigation expenses?

This is to confirm that both activities described above are
permissible under the Act. The Act permits candidates to form
ballot measure committees and to give campaign funds to them,
whether or not they are controlled by other candidates. (Hilasch
Advice Letters No. A-89-533 (copies enclosed); see also Sections
89510, 89512 and 85304.2)

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code.

2 On September 25, 1990, the U.S. District Court, in the case of
Service Employees International Union, et al. V. Fair Political
Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LJH, held that Section
85304 was unconstitutional. However, on September 28, 1990, the
court stayed its judgment as it pertained to candidates for the
California legislature until a "final judgment that the campaign
contribution limitations of Proposition 68 are not effective." No
final judgment the concerning contribution limitations of
Proposition 68 has yet been issued. Until that time, Section
85304 remains in effect as to candidates for the California
legislature.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-90-731
Page 2


     Furthermore, a ballot measure committee may expend its funds
upon matters which have a political, legislative or governmental
purpose. (See generally, Sections 85510-89518.) The Commission
has found expenditures by committees which concern litigation on
initiatives to serve a political purpose. (See Bunt Advice
Letter, No. A-87-301, copy enclosed.)

     Under the facts you have presented, Senator Kopp has been
appearing in court in his capacity as an attorney to defend
Proposition 73 in litigation.3 Based upon the Commission advice
described above, a legislator thus may form a ballot measure
committee to assist in the defense of Proposition 73 and committee
expenditures to reimburse Senator Kopp for the expenses of the
litigation are permissible because they serve a political purpose.
Please note, however, that the committee expenditures may not be
used to reimburse Senator Kopp in violation of Section 89518 if he
is one of the committee's controlling candidates. Based upon the
facts presented, it does not appear that Senator Kopp would be
receiving from the committee anything more than reimbursement for
his out-of-pocket expenses.

     Please contact me if you have additional questions regarding
this matter.

                                          Sincerely,

                                          [signature]
                                          Scott Hallabrin
                                          Acting General Counsel

SH:ken

Enclosures




3    We offer no advice in this letter as to whether campaign funds
may be expended by a ballot measure committee to seek the
imposition of civil penalties against specific persons for
violation of any provisions of an initiative.
